data.title: Rocket Lab: Still A Buy Despite High Valuation
data.description:
Rocket Lab USA (NASDAQ:RKLB) posted its first quarter results on May 9, beating analyst estimates on the top and bottom line. The stock price declined slightly in post-market trading, which to me actually does not mean much especially since the stock price rose 29% since my last report. In that report, I discussed the importance of Rocket Lab being selected for the NSSL Phase 3 contract, enabling it to compete for launch services using its Neutron Rocket.

During the first quarter earnings call, I saw many of the positives that will play out over the mid to longer term being highlighted by management. To me, that's a positive since Rocket Lab is working on something that justifies its stock prices in the long term.

The way I view the company is that it's currently scaling up its business that gives the company some earnings growth momentum, but it's also investing in new products such as Neutron and acquiring new business for its Space Systems segment that will truly unlock new revenue streams that it can build out further.

Obviously, the earnings that the company is achieving currently are important as we do need to see some evidence that its existing business is growing nicely, but I'm particularly upbeat on the company’s longer-term prospects.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings, outlook and significant developments during the quarter. The sequence of the discussion also reflects the importance I put into what Rocket Lab presented from least important to most important. Additionally, I will update my price target for the stock.

Rocket Lab Revenues Drop Sequentially On Lower Launch ASP

First quarter revenues increased 32.1% to $122.6 million. Sequentially, revenues decreased by 7.4%. What I do find somewhat ironic is that in the prior earnings call presentation, Rocket Lab included a sequential growth figure prominently, but apparently when there's a sales decline that does not get featured. I can understand that a company would not want to prominently feature it, but I would think that it is better not to change the presentations in that way.

Gross margins increased by 100 basis points to 28.8% but declined 60 basis points to 33.4% on a non-GAAP basis. Launch revenues increased 9% year-over-year to $35.6 million and declined 16% sequentially. For companies that are in a growth phase, showing sequential development makes sense, but there's seasonality in launches with Q1 usually being soft.

Average sales price per launch declines sequentially on five launches in both quarters. So, the average revenue per launch declined from $8.5 million to $7.1 million. That's not a sign that Rocket Lab has to steeply discount launch prices to get customers. It's mostly a mix effect where multi-launch customers obtain a discount and if we have more multi-launch customers launching in a quarter compared to the other, it may decrease the revenue per launch which is what happened in Q1 2025. Year-on-year, the ASP was also lower, but this was offset by an additional launch in the quarter.

Sequentially, Space Systems sale reduced by $3 million to $87 million, marking a 3.3% decrease but a 45% increase year-on-year as the business is growing through organic and inorganic means.

During the quarter, adjusted EBITDA decreased sequentially from a $23.2 million loss to a $30 million loss while free cash flow burn increased from $23.9 million to $82.9 million driven by a receipt from a Space Development Agency program in Q4.

Rocket Lab Expects Sequential Growth In Q2

For the second quarter, Rocket Lab expects $130 million to $140 million in revenues with year-on-year growth in both segments. Sequentially, the company expects revenues to increase by 6% to 14% with margins of 34% to 36%. This is driven by ASP and launch cadence to improve throughout the quarter and higher output in the Space Systems segment and some sales shifting from Q1 into Q2. Adjusted EBITDA loss is expected to be stable sequentially or $2 million better.

Currently, the company remains loss making as investments in the development of the Neutron rocket are ongoing. So, operational results from Launch Services and Space Systems are offset by required investments for Rocket Lab to bring the Neutron rocket to the market.

What could introduce some softness in the stock price is the fact that analysts are expecting revenues of $137.5 million, which is above the mid-point of the guidance that management provided. However, overall I'm not too concerned given the fact that the company has only missed revenue estimates three times in the past 15 quarters.

Rocket Lab Sees Progress On Business Developments

During the first quarter, Rocket Lab was selected to compete on launch services for the NSSL Phase 3 contract using the Neutron rocket. Previously, I already attested that this is a major sign of confidence in Rocket Launch from the Department of Defense and the value Rocket Lab offers to the DoD to bring defense payloads into orbit. Management also underscored the vote of confidence during the call:

Our selection to it is a huge vote of confidence by the Pentagon and Neutron and affirms us as one of the most capable American launch providers. We are also the only publicly traded company to ever onboard NSSL. Once we are clear of Neutron's first launch, we'll be bidding for task orders under the Phase 3 Lane 1 program which has a total value of $5.6 billion and an ordering period through to June 2029. We've already completed a kickoff meeting with the full contingent of future mission partners including the U.S. Space Force, Assured Access to Space, NRO, Office of Space Launch and other stakeholders from across the government. This was part of a $5 million task order for a mission assurance showcase that came with Neutron selection.

Besides Neutron launch capabilities expecting to go live enabling revenue streams from a different launch payload bracket, Rocket Lab is also seeing increased momentum for its HASTE vehicle for hypersonic test launch capability. The company is already part of the EWAAC (Enterprise-Wide Agile Acquisition Contract) and MACH-TB programs and the company has also been selected by the UK Ministry of Defense for the $1.3 billion framework for hypsonic capabilities.

In March, I discussed the acquisition of Mynaric, which in my view guarantees Rocket Lab access to the terminals it has ordered, bolsters its space products and customer portfolio and could eventually be leveraged for Rocket Lab’s own constellation development.

Unsurprisingly, the company also confirmed my view on the importance on the meaning of the acquisition beyond “just an inorganic growth opportunity”:

Now by bringing them in-house, the terminals in-house we will add a new element to our spacecraft supply chain that improves our product line and strengthens our position in commercial, national security and defense contracts.

Mynaric terminals are already being supplied for a half a billion dollar contract with the Space Development Agency along with many other companies, making this even more of a logical integration. We've proven across all of our acquisitions to date that we can take a highly sought after product, scale it and make it available in high volume. It's our full intention to do the same here again by expanding into Europe and to bring Mynaric terminals to the world and potentially for our own Constellation too. So I'm excited about the potential of this deal and we'll be sure to keep you updated on its progress.

During the quarter, Rocket Lab completed the Stage Flight Hardware and Structures Qualification. So, the second stage will be going through final assembly and be shipped for testing with the engine in the next few months. Stage 1 is close to completion as well which would provide a major milestone completion for the rocket.

The company currently is planning for a launch in the second half of the year. Based on how many items remain to be completed, there remains some risk. Previously the launch was set for mid 2025 and that has now slipped into the second half of 2025. I would not be surprised if a second half launch target indicates a launch very late in 2025. From there, if there's any slip in the schedule, you would be looking at a launch in 2026.

So, there's significant progress, but a lot of work remains to be completed.

Rocket Lab Remains A Buy On Mid-Term Growth Expectations

Rocket Lab still is not a stock that is extremely cheap. I have modeled a price target assessment based on the price-to-sales method and assumed that the acquisition of Mynaric will be settled through shares being issued. For 2025, the stock is definitely overvalued and against FY26 sales, the stock is overvalued by around 4%. Allowing a stock to trade year ahead of its results makes sense to me. So, the current price is actually a fair one and it also is close to the averaged price targets based on the median and minimum price-to-sales for 2027 and in line with the minimum for 2028. That's where I believe the value of the stock is comfortable and those levels are only slightly below the current levels.

However, if we look at where the price could potentially be heading in the years ahead, we see 32% upside for 2027 and stock prices doubling for 2028 with the median and minimum implied stock price targets offering 48% upside, which is my bullish case. So, if you buy now you're definitely not buying at a discount but we also see an opportunity for longer-term upside which I believe continues to support a buy thesis granted you are not hesitant to pay a premium because given the business prospects it is possible that the company will continue to trade at a premium in which case you may miss upside waiting for a more compelling entry point.

The only ways in which I believe we could see a significant reduction in the share prices is in a marketwide sell off or if Neutron rocket development experiences significant delays. The third risk would be losing contracts, most notably the SDA Tranche 2 for which Rocket Lab is under contract to provide part of the satellites, as we do see that the DoD may potentially change course in an effort to cut costs. At the same time, we do see that President Donald Trump is eyeing a $1 trillion security budget. So, there could be a pressure but measured against the opportunities and the market demand commercial and from government customers (not just US Government) the opportunities far outweigh the headwinds.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Remains My Favorite End-to-End Space Company

The Rocket Lab valuation remains stretched and that may result in softer share prices if investors get too accustomed to the achievements of Rocket Lab and price in any potential bad news in more aggressively.

However, I do like the growth in the business, the expansion of the launch capabilities and the acquisition of companies bolstering the product portfolio that can be used to get one step closer to provide complete end-to-end opportunities from satellite design to launch and orbit injection. Furthermore, we see that the DoD is valuing Rocket Lab as a commercial partner giving it a spot to compete for hypersonics testing and compete for defense payload deliveries.

I believe that the stock remains a strong buy on its bright prospects despite its high valuation.

data.title: Zelikovic Investments' Letter (Updated Q1 - 2025)
data.description:
"What has been will be again, what has been done will be done again; there is nothing new under the sun" - Ecclesiastes

Dear Investors,

I have the privilege to write you this annual letter for the past fiscal year of 2024 (updated for May 2025).

As usual, I first wish to welcome the new clients who have joined our financial journey during this past year. It is a great honor and a large responsibility for me to provide you all the financial advice you deserve.

General Financial Overview

Let's begin with a short story with an important moral from the book "Winning Long Term Games" written by my friend, the Italian researcher and writer Luca Dellana. Luca describes his cousin, a young, skilled and famous Italian skier who even made it to the World Championship for his age when he was 6. The boy, considered a very promising talent in Italy, trained with unusual ambition, attained very high speeds and fearless achievements, and went on to excel in various competitions for young skiers. Unfortunately, his career ended abruptly at age 16 due to several injuries, including ankle and knee fractures, causing him to retire at 18. Luca used his cousin's story to argue that skiers who have great skiing careers are not necessarily the fastest, but rather the fastest of those who did not get injured and retire. Luca took the example from sports and applied it to other areas of life, including economics and finance. He reached several financial conclusions noted in some of his books, using the term "Ergodicity". This term provides a method for calculating the outcomes of our behaviors under various scenarios, scientifically proving that we must reach short-term decisions while recognizing that the results have long-term ramifications that may be different from the "average" results. Luca's cousin skied rapidly and fearlessly as a youth, without considering the risk of an injury that might prevent him from further developing his career as an adult. Similarly, if we make investment decisions that consider only the immediate short term without considering the broader future risks, we might make incorrect decisions. This rule serves as our beacon when we issue our investment recommendations.

Similar to 2023, 2024 was an excellent year for most investors. The S&P 500 index (SP500) (SPX) rose 25% and the NASDAQ 100 increased by over 26% (a trend that has sharply reversed in early 2025). The Russell 2000 (RTY) generated a positive return of ~10%. It is important to note that 2024 was the eighth consecutive year in which the Russel 2000 underperformed the S&P 500. The local stock market in Tel Aviv, despite the events that we continue to face us as a nation, was one of the best performing markets in the world for the year 2024, as the Tel Aviv 35 Index rose by 28.4% and the Tel Aviv 125 index increased by 29%.

Your portfolios also enjoyed nice gains in 2024. Ayalon Global Stocks (Israeli Mutual Fund No. 5128855) the fund I act as the sole Advisor, returned a total return of 12.24% in the year 2024. From inception (July 2019) though March 31, 2025, the funds cumulative returns (net of fees) are 105.59% (13.35% CAGR).

For regulatory reasons, we cannot publish a complete comparison of our returns to our benchmarks, but in this link you can find our returns and compare them to the industry average (איילון מניות גלובאלי).

Our fund invests mainly in small/micro market cap shares and our closest benchmark is the Russell 2000, which includes smaller market cap stocks in the U.S. (although we also have significant exposure to stocks which trade outside of the U.S.). Our fund has matched the returns of the S&P 500, although we have no exposure to "The Magnificent Seven" - see below.

We relate our success to several principles that were presented in our previous letters. In a nutshell, these principles include:

  1. Meticulous and informed selection of financially robust companies and purchasing their shares only when they trade at attractive prices compared to their economic value (while steering clear of popular shares that are "glamorous" and expensive compared to their economic value);
  2. Investing in a selected group of global stocks including companies which trade outside of Israel and the U.S.;
  3. Maintaining and retaining a 100% stock portfolio (all upon client discretion, of course) without attempting to time the markets and while avoiding exposure to "alternative" investment tools.

We previously mentioned the significant underperformance of small market cap stocks included in the Russell 2000 as compared to the returns of S&P 500 over the past eight years. In this regard, it is important to note the following finding by Jeff Weniger of Morningstar - in the past, when there was such a big disparity between the two indices, the Russell 2000 followed with extraordinary returns in the ensuing years (1974-1981 and 1999-2008). Indeed, we hope that the current situation will flip, small-cap shares will resume their leadership, and we expect to continue outperforming. To this end, we must be very patient and continue to follow the guidelines above, applying our principles where the objective is not finishing first in every race or always beating the competition, but rather ending a long period of significantly positive returns without taking excess risks along the way. The method we have adopted is one that, historically, has achieved significant outperformance and was implemented by a long line of renowned investors around the world.

We also must address the economic storm brought about this year by Donald Trump's tariff plans. We are not "macro economists" and we seriously doubt the validity of any forecasts made in regards to the outcome of these tariffs and their effects on the stock market. However, we remind you that the American economy has gone through two world wars, a Cold War with Russia, two deep economic recessions, the September 11 attacks, the Covid crisis and a number of more crises. The American and world economies have survived these crises, and the stock market has continued to go up over the long term. So we believe that the markets will overcome the present crisis and continue their long term upward tread.

"The Magnificent Seven", Price/Earnings Multiples and the S&P 500

In last letter for the year 2023, we discussed the stock performance for "The Magnificent Seven" - Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG),(GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA) and Facebook (META). We explained and emphasized the "bubbly" price of these stocks and analyzed the reasons why do not invest in them at these prices. As these stocks continued to be popular in 2024, their contribution to the S&P 500 increased and their profit multiple continued to grow, we wish to update you on our stance regarding these stocks.

About half of the S&P 500's return in 2024 came from the "Magnificent Seven" stocks. Their weight in the S&P 500 grew from 30% in late 2023 to 33% in late 2024. Nvidia's market cap alone rallied over 170% last year, contributing to almost ¼ of the S&P 500 increase in 2024 (interesting anecdote - if you neutralize Nvidia's return, the returns of the S&P 500 over the last 2.5 years are identical to those of the European indexes). No doubt, these are wonderful companies with impressive financial robustness, but their current pricing reflects the expectation that they will continue to grow at the high rates in the distant future. As you recall, as we stated in the past, the challenge seems unsurmountable due to the sheer size of these companies. Usually, for such large companies, retaining high-growth rates over time is a nearly impossible mission. At the end of 2024, the Magnificent Seven traded at a forward price/earnings multiple of ~43.3. Naturally, the higher the multiple paid for a company's profits, the lower the forward return will be. At the end of the year, the entire S&P 500 index traded at a forward price/earning multiple of over 22, largely due to the Magnificent Seven's significant weighting within the index. This P/E multiple is much higher than the historical average multiple of 16.4 (for more information, please read our 2023 letter). And indeed, in early 2025, the Magnificent Seven have dropped (as of the end of the second week of trading in April) by over 18%, causing the S&P 500 to drop by over 10% since the beginning of the year.

The following chart (published by J.P. Morgan Asset Management) reviews the correlation between starting high price/earnings ratio and low S&P 500 future returns. The X-axis shows the future P/E multiples, and the Y-axis shows S&P 500's annual returns (by decades) at different timelines. We can clearly see that as the multiple increases, the returns in the following decade, decline. Furthermore, whenever the index traded on a multiple similar to today's multiple (22; red line on the chart), the range for the annual returns in the following decade were between -2% and +2%. Projected forward, this will lead to future returns that are far from the +20% yields that investors have grown accustomed to in recent years.

In addition to the above, there are other factors which will hinder the Magnificent Seven's ability to maintain their hegemony and continue generating substantial shareholder returns. These factors include:

First, the size of these giant companies. The bigger a company, the greater its peripherals, both geographically and in business terms. Company activities become more diverse and complex, bureaucracy takes over and, concurrently, the CEO/senior management lose the ability to integrate and process all of the incoming information. As a result, company operations begin to "stammer", the ability to innovate is slowed, incorrect decisions are made and growth is halted. Apple, for example, which has a very high weighting in S&P 500 (over 7%) and traded at the end of the year at a very high price/earnings multiple of 30, demonstrated negative profit growth in the last two years. It is not without reason that Warren Buffett continues to divest his holdings in the company.

Second, external competition will threaten these mega companies. Their huge profits inspire many competitors to develop and engage in similar activities in order to enjoy similar profits. As the competitor's technologically develops, it will render the "Magnificent Seven's" advantage irrelevant and respectively impact the value of their shares. In the past that was indeed the case with giant companies such as Blockbuster, Kodak (KODK) and Nokia (NOK). Just recently, the Chinese market launched an AI product (DEEPSEEK) competing with ChatGPT and with Nvidia products. The product generated waves in the global capital market. Over the last few months, Nvidia has already lost $1T of its market cap.

Finally, the status of U.S. companies in the global capital market. Following the impressive rally in the U.S. stock market over the last 15 years, as most stock exchanges around the world were left behind, the market weighting of American companies is ~70% of the global stock index (much higher than the percentage of the American economy out of the total global economy, ~26%). American companies are now much more expensive in comparison to their brethren overseas. The graph below, prepared by Oakmark International, shows the ratio between stock prices in the global market and the price of the American shares (reflected in the ratio between P/E multiples for global and American stocks) over the past two decades. The graph clearly shows how the difference between the high prices of the American stocks and the global stocks has increased over the last decade, currently at 35%. This gap has continued to grow in recent years, propelled by investors investing primarily in the larger American indices. It is important to note that this trend has begun to turn at the beginning of the current year where, as of writing these lines, the European stock index has risen slightly since the beginning of the year and the S&P 500 has declined. In any event, we believe the greatest opportunities for the best long-term investments are within a select group of companies outside of the U.S. (and smaller cap stocks in the U.S).

In this regard, we are reminded of the great quote by Prof. Joel Greenblatt:

"The secret to successful investing is relatively simple: Figure out the value of something and the pay a lot less."

Based on the above, the conclusion is that, as investors, we must avoid chasing the winning stocks (or leading markets) of the recent past (which occurs automatically when we invest in the indices). Instead, we must work on finding quality companies to hold for the long term, especially among small caps and international equities.

Calculating Long-Term Shareholder Returns:

As I do every year, I will dedicate part of this letter to discuss in depth a financial principle. After last year we analyzed the ratio of "return on equity", and the importance of a company's ability to reinvest its profits at a high return and thereby maintaining a high return on equity over time, this year we will analyze the components of the formula for calculating shareholder returns over time.

It is important to emphasize that, in the short term, the price of stocks is quite random, impacted mainly by the whims of the investing public (and U.S. presidents…), but the returns of a stock over the long-term return can be quantified and determined by three main parameters: 1. Growth in company profits; 2. Change in the profit multiple at which company stocks are traded; and 3. Dividends (or share

Total Return= (EPS Growth x Change in P/E multiple) + Dividend/Buyback Yield

We will focus the discussion on two of the three factors above, i.e. growth in a company's profits and the change in the P/E multiple. We will elaborate on the effect of dividends/buybacks in a future letter.

In order to practically use the formula so that percentage data can be multiplied, we must modify it mathematically. This modification includes the conversion of all percentage numbers in the formula to single numbers with the basis of 1=100%. In addition, there is a need to add the number "1" before each percentage number in the formula and deduct "1" at its end. The resulting number is converted back to percentage.

Let's illustrate the formula with an example. Remember, that the P/E multiple is equivalent to a company's market cap divided by its net profit. Let's assume that a company earns $10 per share and trades at a multiple of 10. The share price for this company will be $100. Let's assume further that the company will not pay out dividends over the years, but its profits will double, whereby the earnings per share will be $20 each. If the P/E multiple remains without change, then the share price will double to $200 : ((1=1) X ( (1+ 0))- 1= 1= 100%. In other words, 100% profit. However, if the investors will be impressed by the increase in profits and decide that the company deserves a higher multiple, say 20, the share price will increase respectively to $400, instead of $200. This generates an overall gain of 300% (using the formula ((1+1) X (1+1)) -1 = 3= 300%). A total return of 300%. One must, of course, add dividends and buybacks to the investor's return.

Let's examine a slightly different scenario where company profits remain stable at $10 per share. In this case, investors are disappointed by the lack of growth and decide that the company deserves a multiple of 5 instead of 10. What will happen to the share price? It will decline by 50% (in accordance with the formula above ((1+0) X (1+(-0.5))- 1 = -0.5=-50%)).

Indeed, this is an important principle in the capital market - estimating EPS growth is not enough, one must examine the multiple by which the market will price the company's profits. It is important to emphasize that multiples do not progress in a linear manner. They can drastically increase and decline in a short period of time based on investor risk affinity and their varying expectations for a specific company. The important component over time (and the most dominant and stable element of the formula above) are a company's earnings. The multiple is also critical, but our formula assumes that, over time, the multiple will converge to the historical average for the index/company.

As such, the wise investor will ask: Shouldn't I invest in companies with the highest growth rate (which usually also attain the highest P/E multiple over time)? I focused on this topic in our 2022 letter (see the section on Verdad Capita's research, pg. 7. (I will just add here that, historically, the company's with the highest growth rate that do not usually sustain their growth over time, thus disappointing the investors as growth declines. Concurrently, the multiple that the market is willing to pay for those profits decline, and therefore the share suffer a "double whammy". It is the slower and steadier growers (especially companies with dependable ROE's) of which the market does not expect great surprises that usually prove to be the best investments over time. Also unloved and declining companies or so called- "Value stocks", are more likely to surprise the market (these stock many times are priced for no growth/negative growth and on average have a better chance to surprise the market). Therefore, in principle, these companies usually deliver higher returns than traditional "growth" companies due to the re-rating of their multiples when they do surprise with better-than-expected earnings.

The importance of the interaction between EPS and change in the P/E multiple, reflected in our initial formula, can also be applied to the S&P 500 as a whole. The graph below, published by Distillate Capital, clearly shows that index performance has tracked increased company profits in a relatively stable manner since 1985 (shown as free cash flow). But as stated earlier, there has been a certain detachment in the U.S. in recent years, where the index (blue line) has grown much more than the company's EPS (brown line).

In 2024, for example, when the index rose by over 25%, the aggregate EPS for the S&P 500 increased by only ~8%. The main reason is that, in recent years, the P/E multiple has been growing much more than the growth in profits. However, the multiple that investors willing to pay has increased by ~15% (taken from Voss Capital). The outperformance of the S&P 500 over the past decade is mainly due to the fact that investors were willing to pay much more for U.S. company profits. Only ~25% of U.S. company stock exchange performance can be related to their improved earnings. That takes us back to the discussion that opened this letter: Why do we avoid 100% of the portfolio in the U.S.? When the P/E multiples return to their historical average (a process already underway since early 2025), the U.S. investors will suffer significant losses. Also see the Bizportal article that I co-wrote with my colleague, Gilad Slonim in Hebrew (מה הגורמים לעליות ב-S&P 500, מה קרה בעבר ולמה אפשר לצפות ממנו בעתיד?).

The following table depicts the various components of the long-term shareholder returns formula of all companies in which we are invested, as compared to the corresponding data for all of the S&P 500 companies. Of course, it is very difficult to estimate future growth, but for the formula we used the past 10 years as an indication for future growth and assumed that both for our companies and the companies in the S&P 500 the P/E multiple will revert back to the S&P 500's historic mean P/E of 15.

Our companies have exhibited more earnings growth and trade at lower multiples than the index, which causes us to expect (on average) higher returns from our portfolio vs. the S&P 500 in the future (our Company's also distribute more dividends/buybacks).

Now, let's go on to describe in further detail the companies in our portfolio:

Our Portfolios:

Our portfolios are primarily divided into two categories:

  1. "Compounder" stocks- "Buy and hold".
  2. Micro-Cap value stocks, re-balanced monthly.

Before elaborating on each category, I would like to address a great question a few of our investors asked me - regarding why we apply several strategies to the portfolio and spread our investments among many shares instead of focusing on the 10-20 companies we find most promising? This question is further pertinent in light of the fact that most of the successful value investors in the past and present usually manage highly concentrated portfolios with the largest exposure to their "highest conviction ideas" while objecting to "diworsification". Their method is indeed logical, but we operate differently for several reasons.

First, a highly concentric portfolio (i.e., only 10 stocks) may demonstrate high volatility and a low correlation to the market. Sometimes, a standard deviation that is too high can erode the investors' nerves and lead them to sell their portfolios and divest at the worst time (remember Luca's Italian cousin?). After all, our goal is to assist our partners for the long term and if we can't keep you on course in the short term, our long-term results will not matter. By implementing several internationally dispersed investment strategies and by investing in different industries, we sidestep part of this excess volatility.

Second, even the best analyst will have a very difficult time in predicting which one of his portfolio companies will lead the highest returns (he probably will not admit as much). Consider a recent study by Prof. Hendrik Bessembinder of Arizona State University, he found that about half of all wealth generated by public companies in the U.S. (~$16T) over a period of nearly 100 years (starting in 1926) derived from the returns of only ~90 companies (less than 0.33% of the publicly traded companies). Furthermore, only about 1,000 companies (out of tens of thousands traded on the U.S. stock exchange during this period) were responsible for all wealth created in the American capital market. All other stocks returned, on average, a return similar to that of government bonds, where 57% of the companies returned even less than government bonds. Prof. Bessembinder found similar findings for international stocks as well. From my point of view, this study proves just how few of the world's stocks manage to return a significant return over time, demonstrating how important it is to diversify stock investments in an appropriate manner in order to enable exposure to some, even if not all, of the successful shares. The chance of us knowing in advance which shares will be the big winners is small, so we diversify our investments between a good number of stocks, countries, and industries. This is not to say that we will buy a broad collection of stocks, as do those investing in stock indices, but we have adopted a middle ground of ~100 select companies, most of which are led by excellent management bodies that specialize in capital allocation. What we want is for these managers to continue investing their companies' profits in an informed and profitable manner.

Another important lesson to be learned from Hendrik Bessembinder's study is that most wealth created in the capital market was generated by buying and holding a small number of shares. Unlike many investment managers who tend to sell or trim their holdings after a small profit, we tend to sell any shares, we rather "stick" to the Compounder stocks that we have chosen. Once we buy into a company, except in rare cases, we will not reduce our holdings, and we will allow the stock to rise over time so long as the pricing is not too extreme compared to the company's financials. These successful stocks will grow and naturally become a larger part of our investment portfolio. Indeed, as of today, despite the broad and evenly weighted distribution of our initial investments, five of our "successful" stocks (TVK, FFH, TPL, KKR and Protector) already make up a large part of our investment portfolio (where our "less" successful investments currently have a much smaller weighting). We also have several new smaller positions which we believe may become much larger positions due to compounding over time.

In this regard, this past winter I gave a talk at ValueX, a wonderful investment conference hosted by Guy Speir, in Klosters, Switzerland. The company I wished to pitch to the respectable audience was one of our largest holdings, Texas Pacific Land Trust. When I began preparing the stock pitch I realized it may be a difficult task to convince an audience of value investors to buy a stock which traded at the time a P/E multiple of above 60. Looking for a convincing argument why I am still willing to hold such an expensive stock I came across the following quote from Charlie Munger:

". . . I have a defect. And I just won't pay 30 times earnings… I have never done that but I have one or two now which are now worth 8 or 10 times what I paid for them and they are still marvelous businesses and are still growing and I just hold them. …. I don't defend this logic. I just say this is the way I do it and it keeps me more comfortable to do it this way."

Reading Munger's words triggered me to ask a question: "is this method indeed illogical?", and stimulated me to test the hypothesis that "buying and holding" carefully selected stocks for the long term is a legitimate and logical investment pathway. Is it indeed not better to buy the stock of a financially strong and well-managed company at a good price and then hold it for the long term even if it becomes pricey? To test this hypothesis, we first selected the ten largest and most famous, publicly-traded, stocks Buffett and Munger held in Berkshire over the years[1]. We then examined what would have been the returns of Buffett and Munger if instead of "buying and holding" these stocks, as they did over the years, they had adapted a more aggressive strategy whereby after they purchased a stock they would have sold it every time it became expensive and only bought it back if it became cheap again. To define cheap/expensive we used the P/E ratio and assumed all stocks were cheap when they traded below a P/E ratio of 15 and sold completely as expensive stocks only when they traded at a P/E ratio higher than 30 (a scenario similar to Buffett's Apple stock trade).

In our simulation of the hypothesis conditions, we used the following parameters (all historical data were taken from YCharts and Yahoo Finance):

  1. All stocks were bought and held from the moment Berkshire purchased them (even if they were purchased at a P/E ratio higher than 15) and until the stock traded above a P/E ratio of 30 at which time we assumed Berkshire would have sold the position entirely and paid taxes.
  2. When a stock was sold the net proceeds were invested either in the S&P 500 or in Berkshire's stock.
  3. The stock would only be purchased again if its P/E ratio would drop below 15; otherwise the investment would remain in the S&P 500 or in Berkshire's stock.

We then compared the CAGR actually achieved for each stock over the years by the "buy and hold" strategy used at Berkshire with the CAGR achieved under the simulation conditions of "buy and sell", namely during a time period in which the stock would be invested in the S&P 500 or as a Berkshire's stock, due to its higher P/E ratio. For example, in the case of Coca-Cola (KO), instead of holding the stock for 36 years (from 1987 until today) Berkshire would have held it only for 15.5 years because the stock was sold when it reached a P/E ratio of 30 and bought back only when it was traded at a P/E ratio under 15. Our findings are summarized in the Table in the Appendix.

The results of our study were striking and in line with our hypothesis. With the exception of Coca-Cola and Bank of America stocks, the CAGR after taxes for all other companies' stocks was markedly higher for the "buying and holding" strategy (average of 11.25%) than for the "buying and selling" pathway, in both the S&P 500 (8.5%) and the Berkshire stock (8.8%) alternatives

For us, the message from this exercise is clear - buying and holding carefully - selected companies is a very logical strategy, even when these stocks become a bit pricey. The findings of our initial, small-scale study should be supported by future large-scale studies .

We wish to conclude this chapter with another quote from Charlie Munger, who once said:

"Investing is mostly about sitting and thinking, and of the two, the sitting is more important".

Well, after such a long introduction, how did our stocks do in 2024?

This year, too, we will begin our "Compounders" (the largest portion of our portfolios):

  1. Compounders

Our Compounders are stocks of companies operating in various fields including many public insurance companies worldwide that are exceptionally profitable, are managed by honest and reliable managers with a proven track record, and have demonstrated their ability to continuously reinvest their profits over time (they exhibit high and consistent return on equity over time). We attempt to purchase shares of these companies (at a fair price) and hold them over time, allowing them to reinvest their profits each year and generate a high return on equity for us over time (our version of the "Coffee Can Portfolio").

Let's begin with the Italian holding company, EXOR (EXO.MI), a longstanding family-owned company (and one of our first holdings (we started buying when the share price was ~€30 and today the stock trades above ~€80) demonstrating excellent capital allocation and very successful acquisitions. The company's current market cap is above ~€26B, despite the past few years being very challenging. Among EXOR's holdings are Ferrari (RACE), Stellantis (STLA), heavy farm equipment company CNH Industrial (CNHI), Juventus Football Club (JUVE.MI), luxury footwear company, Christian Louboutin and The Economist newspaper. The company's net asset value increased by 9% in 2024. There is no doubt that this company is economically robust, firmly complying with all of the criteria I believe in for long-term investments (see my article on EXOR in my blog in The Marker dated 9 April 2020, פתיח איטלקי מוצלח לתיק ההשקעות שמתחת למזרן - רווחים במחשבה תחילה)

Another company we believe belongs in our Compounder bucket is Brookfield Corporation (BN), a Canadian investment management company that controls a number of subsidiaries, such as Brookfield Asset Management (which we also hold), Brookfield Reinsurance and other companies engaged in the energy, real estate and finance sector. Led by the legendary CEO, Bruce Flatt, the company's stock price increased by an average of 19% since 2002 when he became CEO. Brookfield continues to increase the AUM (Assets Under Management) on average by ~20% per annum. The company has also recently announced the appointment of the young and promising Connor Teskey who will be taking over as CEO instead of Bruce. As company shareholders, we share in the management and success fees that they charge their clients. The stock value in the parent company, BN, increased by over 50% in 2024, concurrently with an almost 20% increase in the company's earnings. This company aims to adopt the Berkshire Hathaway model, and we will be their very satisfied partners if they succeed in doing so.

We also hold InterActiveCorp (IAC) in the tech sector, controlled by the famous investor Barry Diller. Its stock value declined by another ~18% last year, but this decline disregards the immense value that the company generated for shareholders over the years. Joey Levin recently resigned from managing the company (and went on to manage the subsidiary, ANGI), but we trust the senior managers led by Barry Diller to continue navigating the ship. Company holdings including Dotdash Meredith (the American media corporation) and many private companies. This company's excellent management has proven its ability to save its subsidiaries from crises. Due to the high discount at which the company trades compared to its Net Asset Value we once again increased our exposure to this company at a bargain price.

We also increased our position in the giant American-global asset management company Kohlberg, Kravis, Roberts (KKR & Co.), which currently manages ~$640B (reflecting an annual growth rate of 18% since its IPO in 2010). Concurrently, the company's stock has risen at a CAGR of over 20% per annum since 2010. The stocks also rose by over 80% in 2024 (with a sharp decline in early 2025, which we utilized to purchase even more shares). I believe that the stock still trades at a substantial discount compared to its long-term potential and due to the fact that the asset management industry is among the best and most lucrative industries .

We remind you this year again that we believe that one of the most promising pathways for profitable investments in the stock market is investing in insurance companies. A well-managed insurance company has two sources of profitability: 1. Insurance activity, and 2. Investment of its "float" (see my Seeking Alpha article from November 2016 on this subject). Accordingly and after careful examination, we have increased our stake in a number of insurance companies, including Fairfax Financial Holdings (FFH.TO), which has compounded book value at an almost 18% rate for the past 30 years, and its stock has also returned a CAGR of nearly 18% p.a. over the last 30 years, including an increase of 63% in 2024 (following an increase of over 60% in 2023 as well) and whose investment portfolio has grown to $66B while retaining a high level of profitability in its insurance operations. Included in the list are also the excellent Assured Guaranty (AGO, a company insuring mainly municipal liabilities), American Markel Corporation (MKL- a holding/insurance company that many call "Baby Berkshire"), managed by the renowned investor Thomas Gayner and the Norwegian insurance company Protector, which continues to star in the European insurance industry. When we began buying its shares in 2017, they traded a ~60 Krona (about $6 USD) and its current value exceeds 360 Krona, a return over 5 times on our original investment (an annual return of nearly 60% per annum. These four companies have continued to demonstrate excellent economic performance in 2024 as their stock price continued to rise. It seems that these companies will continue to enjoy the multiple opportunities derived of the new interest environment. We have also added Apollo Global Management to this category as it, in addition to owning an insurance company (Athene) is also a known asset management company (manages ~$750B).

This year, we have also purchased American Kinsale Capital Corp (KNSL) to this bucket (thanks to Shreekkanth Viswanathan "Shree" from SVN Capital for first introducing me to this amazing company). It specializes in issuing insurance policies in unique areas that other insurance companies refuse to insure (Excess and Surplus), mainly for small businesses. This company excels in an extraordinary low loss ratio, and its shares have not lagged behind as since its IPO in 2016, their shares have risen at a CAGR of nearly 50%.

Texas Pacific Land Trust's (TPL) stock continued to rise and rose by over 120% in 2024! This company remains very profitable and will continue enjoying royalties from its oil and water reservoirs in Texas. In addition, there is a high probability that data centers will be built on its land, an activity that may generate many million of additional profits per annum in the future. We intend to hold on to it for many years, along with our other Compounders.

The Israeli representative in our portfolio, the stocks of the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange (TASE), did not disappoint in 2024. Led by CEO Itai Ben Zeev, its stock was one of the best performers in the local exchange in 2024, returning above 120%, due to a 27% increase in its earnings per share! Tase also completed a buyback of its shares (de facto increasing the holdings of all company shareholders) from the American Manikay Global (which remained with a 15% holding in TASE). We believe that this buyback will prove to itself over the years as further business improvements are introduced at TASE.

New Compounders

In 2024, I recommended adding several stocks to the investment portfolio, two of which I would like to discuss below.

The first company is a British company, Burford Capital (BUR), a leader in litigation financing. The company conducts a unique activity manifested in exceptional profitability. It earns money by purchasing all or part of the claimants' rights to a legal claim, taking over for or joining the claimants, leading the claim and winning all or part of the claim amount at the end of the process. By so doing, the company enables the claimant to "meet" the money early and achieve a higher certainty of success. Burford takes over as a partner to the claim (and the risks), manages it and, in most cases, improves its chances of success thanks to its skilled legal team. Burford owns many claims and it also manages 8 investment funds that raise money from the public. These funds currently manage over $3B, funding the acquisition of additional claims (all of these funds are owned by the public parent company). Company management operates exceptionally and, over the years, they have wisely invested in successful claims, thus its high profitability. The company's flagship claim, currently in advance stages, was filed against the Argentinean government for nationalizing a public company (YPF) in 2012 without compensating the investors. Burford is entitled to ~35% of the claim. The U.S. court has already handed down a ruling against the Argentinean government in this case (subject of appeal) and it is currently awaiting a ruling on the amount of damages. Applying conservative estimates, Burford's share in the future damages is ~ַ£6B, double Burford's current market cap (and, as stated, it has several other claims "in the works").

It is more convenient to write only about the successful investments, but it is important to present and learn from the investments that disappointed us. I will demonstrate this well with our recommendation to invest in Atkore Inc (ATKR) over the past year, an American company operating in the industrial sector. The company has been operating for over 60 years, engaged in two main sectors - electrical safety and infrastructure. What is unique in this company (in addition to the bargain price at which we purchased its stock) is its outstanding management, which excels in capital allocation and invests a large portion of its profits in company share buybacks (at bargain prices) and in acquiring other companies. When crunching the numbers, the company reported especially high profitability rates, with a 50.3% average "Return On Equity" in the last ten years (an average S&P 500 company currently reports ~16%). Despite that and mainly because the company is involved in a cyclical industry, company shares traded at a low P/E multiple of 8 when we bought it. But since we bought it, the company shares continued to decline by almost 50% due to poor financial statements that disappointed the investors. Company share continued to decline along with all companies in the construction sector due to investor concern with the economic slowdown in the U.S. and its current P/E multiple is just ~6.

This being the case, we were faced with three options - completely sell the position (and admit the "mistake"), increase the investment or not touch it at all. Experiencing a decline in a short period of time is indeed "unpleasant", but we must always stay rational, examine the company's economics and avoid focusing solely on its share price. In this case, the company is still maintaining a high "Return on Equity" (~20%), but its current market cap reflects it well and that is why it is traded at such a low P/E multiple. Concurrently, the company announced another substantial share buyback. As such, we recommend continued exposure to the company, and we've even decided to increase our investment in it.

Let's end this section with a pertinent quote by American Nobel Prize Laureate in Economics, Paul Samuelson, which we adopted in our financial path:

"Investing should be dull, it shouldn't be exciting; investing should be more like watching paint dry or grass grow"

  1. Micro-Cap Value Stocks

This investment strategy is based on one of the best strategies in the book "What Works on Wall Street" by James P. O'Shaughnessy. This strategy selects a basket of 25 stocks whose market cap at the time of purchase is under 500 million dollars (stocks which are usually excluded from the major indexes in the U.S.A.). These stocks are selected only if they fulfill a number of criteria (including value, momentum and financial stability) which have been proven by empirical studies to be a source of alpha primarily among micro-cap stocks.

As we have witnessed in the first years, some of these stocks and this strategy do not outperform the market every year (including 2024). Nevertheless, this instability per se (which may cause other investors to abandon the strategy at exactly the wrong time) is the key for its success over time, as it involves "going against the herd".

Net Net Stocks

We added another investment strategy to our portfolios. While this strategy is similar to the investment strategy I described above, in this strategy we adhere specifically to a policy of investing in specific stocks called "net-net" stocks. This strategy was first applied by Benjamin Graham (the author of "The Intelligent Investor"), and it involves investing in stocks that trade at a price which is less than the company's net asset value. The market cap of "Net-Net" companies' is lower than the value of all the company's cash and current assets after deducting all of the company's liabilities. Companies that trade at such low prices, usually operate in "out of favor"" industries. Due to their small market value, most of the shares of the companies that meet this criterion will be out of the investment radar of most investors in the world. These days we find many ''Net-Net'' stocks in Asia (especially Japan), Europe and other countries. This strategy has been back-tested in many countries and has so far been proven to yield an excess return to market indices of about 10% per year on average over time (there is of course no guarantee for the future). Graham himself used this strategy and managed to yield an annual return of almost 20% net for his investors.

On the personal level, as your portfolio manager, I am pleased to share with you some of my activities in the year 2024:

  1. We held our first Investors Meeting in July 2024 in Tel Aviv where we had the honor of meeting many of you (almost 300 people attended). The attendees listened to lectures of Gilad Slonim (Financial Planner), our guest Assaf Eden (Advisor to the Eden Discovery Fund) and a lecture by yours truly called "How We Do Not Invest".
  2. My colleague Gilad Slonim and I continue our weekly podcast in Hebrew ( "Investing with Value"- השקעות עם ערך"") where we discuss the careers and strategies of the great value investors. The podcast can be found on here - (השקעות עם ערך - גלעד סלונים ויואב זליקוביץ - פרק #1 - בנג'מין גראהם. האב המייסד של השקעות הערך),and Spotify- )השקעות עם ערך. We had the privilege of hosting a number of renowned investors on the podcast such as William Green, Guy Spier, Prof. Wesley Gray, Arnold Van Der Berg (a successful fund manager and Holocaust survivor) and Shreekkanth Viswanathan, "Shree", from SVN Capital.
  3. I was invited to attend The FatAlpha Investing Conference in Cyprus hosted by Sophocles Sophocles. It was a great experience to meet such smart investors from all over the world. I also gave a short talk at the event where I pitched to the audience our investment in TASE (the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange).
  4. I was invited to attend (2024 and 2025) and speak (2025) at the aforementioned ValueX conference in Klosters, Switzerland .
  5. I was invited to speak to the Capital Markets Club at the Reichman University in Israel.
  6. I published over the past years numerous articles in leading financial portals in Israel and abroad, including The Marker, Bizportal, Globes, Forbes and Seeking Alpha.
  7. I still enjoy the warm environment and cooperative work with the staff of Interactive Brokers. This cooperation is very beneficial for us and for our clients. Special thanks go out to Meirav and Lior Shemesh and their staff, David Shem Tov, Itzik Shemesh and Roee Ben Haim.
  8. We have continued our cooperation with Meitav Dash and due to the high demand for our services with I.R.A.s we began working with another company, GlobalNet, in Israel.

In conclusion, I wish to summarize again and share with you our "Ten Commandments" which underlie and direct us in our investment activities:

  1. Focus on high quality micro-cap value stocks which have a well-documented track record of achieving alpha and not on high-flying popular stocks.
  2. Identify and analyze the best Compounders in the world and invest in them as partners for the long term.
  3. Set a long-term goal of at least 5 years to achieve investment returns.
  4. Utilize the powerful effect of compound interest on long-term investments on behalf of our investors.
  5. Refrain from any speculation and short-term trading.
  6. Not allow "noisy" headlines and catchy newsflashes about the stock market dictate our activity in this market.
  7. Stay away from any investment fads, the long-term economic value of which is highly questionable.
  8. Carefully analyze the past and present investment strategies of the world's best investors, who are renowned for excellent financial performance.
  9. Continuously read and study professional/academic papers, reports and research in the area of investments/finances published around the world.
  10. Finally - rehash to our investors the message- "Patience is gold".

We wish to thank again our investors for the pleasure we get from working with you and for the trust you grant us. On our part, we will continue to work on your behalf in a fair, diligent, professional and trustworthy manner, while utilizing our investing strategy outlined above.

As usual, I wish to thank my friends and partners, Matan Guetta and Liran Younger (who became a father for the first time this year) without whom this path we have set off would not have been possible and definitely not as enjoyable!

Of course, I wish to thank my precious wife, Revital, without whom all of this would not be possible and my two beautiful children, Yehonathan and Libbie, who make sure I come home with a smile.

Finally, I wish to also thank and send my love and admiration to my father, Prof. Israel Zelikovic, who edited and revised this Annual Letter, and of course to my loving mother, Orly, both of whom continue to be role models for me and for our whole family. G-d willing, may they be healthy for many years!

Sincerely,

Yoav Zelikovic

Attorney and Senior Equity Analyst

data.title: Hit To Owens Corning Stock Price Overdone
data.description:
Bigger Picture

This week’s free fall of Owens Corning (NYSE: OC) makes the stock a potential buy opportunity that retail value investors should address with sincere due diligence. Investors were most shaken by the Fed’s commitment to holding interest rates to fight stagflation and, concomitantly, management’s lacklustre near-term outlook for revenue growth. After one day, the stock popped +5% higher, then pulled back to close 3.87% higher.

The stocks of building and construction materials companies have been sensitive, even volatile, to news. Materials stocks have been poor performers this past year, the victims of political and economic straitjackets. Owens Corning investors can expect the stock to hit higher average target prices, as forecasted by analysts, when the Fed lowers interest rates and lenders effectuate better borrowing conditions. Cautious optimism is now the tone and tenor of political and economic talk.

America is going to have to invest in rebuilding its infrastructure. Think of Newark Airport or the crumbling, unsafe roads, described by Transportation for America’s recent revelations. It is only a matter of time before the stock of building and construction materials manufacturers and suppliers stopsunderperforming. Infrastructure stocks are selling at prices attractive enough to offer long-term potential growth opportunities for retail value investors, according to a report card posted by Seeking Alpha on April 29, ’25.

We have been covering building and construction materials companies for Seeking Alpha members and followers over the past 6 months, usually suggesting buy opportunities. Owens Corning is an American legacy business, an iconic roofing and insulation products brand that fits our metrics, leaning toward a buy assessment. In 2024, Owens Corning claims to have acquired for almost $4B the renowned Masonite International business to expand its “growth platform” in the “interior and exterior doors and door systems” sector of the building and construction materials industry. Management has made 4 acquisitions since 2020. 62% of 14 M&As in totalare in building materials, and 31% in manufacturing.

Before the most recent stock price plunge, analysts were equally divided between rating the stock a hold and buy in the last 90 days, according to Seeking Alpha. Its Quant Rating remains a hold but leans to buy:

Valuation and Other Upsides

Seeking Alpha raised the stock's Factor Grade back to B+, as it was graded 6 months earlier, up from D+ three months ago. Owens Corning's valuation metrics exceed those of the Industrial sector median, notably per PE/GAAP, EV/EBITDA, PRICE/SALES, and PRICE/CASH FLOW. One note of risk. The company's Enterprise Value of $16.84 B exceeds its Market Cap of $ 11.52 B, suggesting investors need to consider the stock's volatility, debt, and the share price before investing. In our opinion, the dividend yield adds little to the stock's valuation; the potential for investors lies in the revenue and earnings growth potential.

There are other upsides, too. Shares sell about 15% higher than their 52-week low; short interest is only at 1.34%. Bad news per the Q1’25 earnings announcement on May 7, ’25, has been factored into the drop in the stock price. The shares dived from $214 in November ’24, after a 247% run-up over 5 years, to ~$130 on May 7th. The stock has also suffered from economic stress, i.e., fears of a recession, tariffs, and subsequent stagnation in the housing and construction markets, in part, from high interest rates. European and U.K. banks have been lowering interest rates, and President Trump is pressing the Fed to follow suit. We expect a lowering of rates by the Fed to pop building supply stocks higher.

Multiplying the 9.83 PE ratio by FY’24 EPS of $15.83, a fair value price tops $155 per share. Multiplying a 9.8 PE ratio by an average anticipated FY’25 EPS of $14.70, a fair value price per share equals $144. An April ‘25 survey of analysts forecast an average target price from $176 to over $245 per share. According to Seeking Alpha's latest analysis, analysts estimate the Owens Corning average target share price will hit $171 this year.

For 9 quarters, Owens Corning beat analysts' EPS estimates, which suggests that investors will benefit in the long run. Q1’25 beat estimates by $0.31, revenue by ~$68M on net sales of about $ 11 B.

In February ’25, Fitch upgraded Owen Corning’s credit facility ratings, reassuring that “The Rating Outlook is Stable.” Debt totals about $5.62B. Fitch went on to point out the company enjoys strong credit metrics, ”the Masonite acquisition adds some strategic value… (and) expands Owen Corning’s overall footprint,” the company margins (gross profit ~30% and net income margin 5.9%) are strong and consistent, it’s product diversification adds value and strengthens its market position and market share. Management appears to focus on a “disciplined capital allocation framework.”

We do not foresee additional headwinds causing any further significant drop in the share price.

Downsides

On the downside, the Q1’25 announcement reported net sales increased 25% Y/Y, including$540M in revenue from the newly acquired door business. Net earnings attributable to Owens Corning fell from $278M in Q1’24 to $255M in Q1’25. The diluted EPS was $3.16 in Q1’24 and $2.95 in Q1’25. The adjusted EPS also dropped to $2.97 from $3.40. Operating cash flow and free cash flow fell.

Owens Corning has lost momentum, the 1.94% dividend yield is unattractive in this economic environment, and insider selling outpaced their share buys in the last year. Moreover, growth over the next few quarters is forecast by management to be stagnant. The CEO told shareholders on May 7 to expect revenue growth in “high single digits” in Q2’25, roofing in the low single digits, “insulation revenue is forecasted to decline mid-single digits” Y/Y, and “doors revenue is expected to increase slightly…” as reported by Seeking Alpha. Though Owens Corning is profitable, the firm’s debt-to-equity ratio ending December ’24 was 1.11 compared to the construction industry median of 0.43, adding downward pressure on the stock.

The company has maintained a steady pace of stock buybacks. However, it has a low dividend payout ratio of 15.73%; under current circumstances, we do not foresee the board increasing the payout ratio. A concern, but not a downside, is that the stock has been volatile; the 24-monthBeta is 1.29 and 1.43, currently.

Takeaway

We believe the stock is oversold and a buy opportunity at its current price of ~$130 per share. Growth will come as the nation’s economic stresses ease, mortgage and borrowing rates are cut, and the administration implements its policy position that the nation’s infrastructure needs rebuilding. In our opinion, Owens Corning is a healthy company with upside potential for retail investors to consider building their portfolios.

data.title: RLJ Lodging Preferred: A Strong Candidate Despite Its Optionality Risk
data.description:
RLJ Lodging Trust CUM CONV PFD A (NYSE:RLJ.PR.A) is in focus as we expand our preferred share coverage by looking at a highly touted real estate investment trust's convertible preferred shares. RLJ's series A preferred provides plenty of scope for assessment given its optionality feature. Moreover, RLJ Lodging Trust (NYSE:RLJ) recently released its first-quarter earnings results, adding additional latitude for analysis, especially as fundamental shifts have occurred in the real estate sector.

Without delay, here's what we think of RLJ Lodging Trust CUM CONV PFD A's prospects.

RLJ.PRA's Optics and Recent Performance

RLJ Lodging's Series As are cumulative and convertible, meaning, unless default or similar events occur, investors will receive a set dividend, either incrementally or on a delayed basis. As for its convertible option, RLJ allows preferred shareholders to convert into ordinary shares (expanded upon later), adding flexibility to its risk-return profile.

Salient data shows that RLJ Lodging's Series A's have a liquidation value of $25 (issuer's call date), about 3.20% above the vehicle's current market value (measured on May 8th). In addition, RLJ Lodging's Series As possess a current yield of around 8.06%, which reflects a near 390 basis points spread over than the U.S. 10-year treasury yield. Overall, we see value in the share's dividend yield. However, we think call risk has emerged; RLJ Lodging's Series A is trading awfully close to its liquidation value and refinancing of its initial 7.80% coupon might be lucrative if U.S. interest rates settle lower and/or Series A exceeds its liquidation value.

Metric Value
Market Price (08/04/2025) $24.20
Liquidation Value $25
Call Date 9/1/2017
Current Yield (08/04/2025) 8.06%
Initial Yield 7.80%

Source: Preferred Stock Channel - Headline and Call Feature

A closer look at its convertible option illustrates that RLJ Lodging's Series A preferred shares have a conversion ratio of 0.2806. According to our calculations, the ratio translates into a preferred stock parity price (explanation here) of around $2.02, which is below RLJ Lodging's common stock value of around $7.20, recorded intraday on May 8th. Considering the disparity between the conversion value and the common stock market value, we don't see these shares being worth converting, nor do we see an embedded premium. In essence, we think RLJ Lodging Series A will trade like a regular callable preferred share for the foreseeable future.

Metric Value
Conversion Ratio 0.2806
Parity Price (08/04/2025) $2.02

Source: Preferred Stock Channel - Conversion details. Aside: To reiterate, the parity price was determined intraday on May 8th, it is a fluid calculation.

RLJ Lodging's Series A's price behaviour suggests the stock usually trades near its par value, except during distressed economic periods, namely the great financial crisis where the stock slumped to around $3.30 per share and COVID-19 where a bottom of around $14.06 was priced in. Many preferred shares, especially in the REIT space lost telling value since the U.S. started cutting interest rates last year, those losses extended into 2025 amid higher credit risk and term premiums. That said, RLJ Lodging's Series A shares have held their value quite well, shedding just over 5% in the past six months.

A historical look at the REIT's dividend profile shows that its Series A's have enjoyed consistent dividends aside from a period following the great financial crisis. The cumulative effect has played a critical part, and we think the survival during COVID-19 proves that the REIT's Series As can handle moderate-to-heavy tail risk.

RLJ Lodging: A Fundamental Overview

Operating as a lodging REIT can be challenging because mark-to-market leases are inconsistent and seasonal effects often dampen consistency. A baseline vantage point shows that RLJ Lodging has 94 properties and nearly 21, 000 rooms. In addition, the REIT seems well-capitalised with a net debt-to-enterprise value of 0.56x, which we deem relatively low for a lodging REIT.

A more thorough look at RLJ Lodging's portfolio reveals valuable data points. The REIT's Q1 occupancy settled at 69.1%, which is materially above its usual industry standard. For example, Lightbox recently communicated an expected hospitality occupancy rate of 63.38% for 2025. Furthermore, RLJ REIT's comparable hotel EBITDA margin is arguably healthy at 26.1%, especially if combined with the fact that RLJ Lodging's daily rate increased by $4.27 year-over-year.

RLJ Lodging's first-quarter results were progressive, as the REIT reported $0.31 in funds from operations per share and revenue of $328.1 million, beating both estimates. Looking ahead, management expects disparity in its financial targets. However, regardless of whether you look at a base case or a worst-case scenario, RLJ's outlook seems bright. The vehicle's fiscal 2025 adjusted FFO is forecasted to settle between $209.5 million and $239.5 million, conveying solid economic profitability expectations. Moreover, net income is predicted to range between $34.9 million and $62.9 million, suggesting RLJ REIT's liquidity and solvency risk remains low.

What's our outlook here?

We've argued for an extensive period that we think the U.S. is in the latter stage of its short-term economic cycle. Data sources as well as ourselves believe a cyclical downturn is likely, especially as global trade tensions have diminished trade, consumer, and business sentiment.

Having said that, RLJ Lodging Trust CUM CONV PFD A is a preferred stock, and therefore the emphasis should probably be on RLJ REIT's liquidity and solvency as opposed to its interim growth prospects. Broad-based U.S. corporate credit risk is rising, but we think RLJ REIT is best-in-breed with the necessary attributes to see its solvency bypass economic headwinds. Moreover, RLJ has experienced overlooked pockets of operational growth. For example, its Philadelphia and San Jose properties experienced revenue per available room growth of 26.4% and 14.1% during Q1. If sustained, such pockets of growth could lead to a revised enterprise-wide earnings multiple, in turn, supporting the REIT's preferred share trajectory.

In essence, RLJ REIT can be sensitive to economic cycles, but we think preferred, and ordinary shareholders might experience polarised outcomes in the event of an economic slowdown, where Series A sustains its dividend and provides investors with a unique opportunity to buy at a discount. In contrast, the REIT's ordinary shares don't guarantee a fixed dividend and don't have set liquidation values. As long as solvency risk remains astray, we see Series A prospering.

A Noteworthy Mention

This is somewhat of a residual factor but a worthwhile mention nonetheless.

I recently realised PFFA ETF's (PFFA) notable exposure to RLJ REIT. PFFA ETF is an actively managed ETF that seeks opportunistic income and capital gains opportunities. The fund has delivered telling returns since its inception, beating the U.S. S&P Preferred Share Index by approximately 3.48% per annum since its vintage in 2018. As such, we see PFFA ETF's commitment to RLJ Lodging Trust's preferred shares as a reference point for our bullish outlook.

Final Word

Despite trading near its liquidation value and possessing no noticeable conversion benefits, we think RLJ Lodging Trust CUM CONV PFD A is a good buy. With a yield of above 8%, a history of dividend consistency, and low solvency risk, it seems likely that RLJ Lodging Trust CUM CONV PFD A will produce quality throughout-the-cycle returns.

Consensus: 'Buy'/Sector Outperform

data.title: Stocks Face Their Most Dangerous Moment Yet
data.description:
Stocks have made an impressive move higher but are now at a pivotal point. The current location in the S&P 500 (SP500) and the NASDAQ (COMP:IND) may be the ultimate test, with the 200-day moving average in play and multiple technical levels. To this point, markets have been unable to overcome those challenges.

For the S&P 500, the region between 5,550 and 5,700 is proving to be very challenging. The overhang of the 200-day moving average and the 61.8% retracement of the decline are also challenges. Additionally, there is strong resistance from the July 2024 highs.

The image for the NASDAQ 100 (NDX) is the same, but slightly different. The NASDAQ 100 has also found resistance at the 200-day moving average, the 61.8% retracement level, and resistance that goes back to the June 2024 levels. But more interestingly, the NASDAQ 100 has also now failed at the 200-day moving average four times. That seems to suggest that the 200-day moving average is a tough resistance zone.

Similarities to 2022

We saw something similar to this in the spring of 2022, and eventually, the NASDAQ 100 was unable to get above the 200-day. Additionally, at that time, the NASDAQ 100 was also challenging the 61.8% retracement. The NASDAQ 100 eventually fell by 27% after failing to rise past those two resistance points.

Today, the NASDAQ 100 is closely following the 2022 bear market path. This does mean it has to continue, but it is worth pointing out the many similarities between today's market and the market of 2022.

The same is true for the S&P 500, which is tracking the bear market of 2022 closely so far. This doesn't mean that the market of 2025 will continue to trade with the market of 2022, but it could be sending us important messages about the type of market we are in. One could also argue that it is tracking along with the bear market of 2008, as shown below.

Bear Market or Not?

This could mean that we are either in the early innings of a bear market or not, and the next significant move will likely determine whether the market will make new highs. There is certainly fundamental evidence to suggest that the S&P 500 is overvalued. Earnings estimates for 2025 have declined, and the growth rate has fallen, while the P/E ratio remains elevated at over 21.

BTIC S&P 500 total return futures have collapsed, indicating that equity financing costs are falling, suggesting a lack of demand for leverage or just flat-out unwinding of positions. Interestingly, in 2022, rallies in the S&P 500 were not confirmed by a rise in these futures contracts. This was the case in March and August 2022. When the market finally bottomed in October 2022, the rising cost of equity confirmed the rise in the cash index. So far in 2025, we are seeing equity financing costs continue to decline despite rising stock prices, which is very similar to what was seen in 2022.

We have also seen cross-currency basis swap spreads turn more negative in recent days. Historically, these dollar funding cost measures have tracked changes in the S&P 500. This is a sign that funding costs for dollars are growing more expensive, and liquidity levels are tightening somewhat.

If the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ 100 can meaningfully breach the 200-day moving average, they can stay above it for a few days. Then, one must consider that this is not a bear market because, despite the negative sentiment and fundamental readings, the patterns of bear markets in the past have been broken.

However, the declines could be fierce if the indexes fail to push higher here.
